= Danish Gambit Accepted, Classical Defence =

=== 5...Nf6 ===
Black's knight on f6 is targeting the undefended e-pawn. Turning the tables with '''[[Chess Opening Theory/1. e4/1...e5/2. d4/2...exd4/3. c3/3...dxc3/4. Bc4/4...cxb2/5. Bxb2/5...Nf6/6. e5|6. e5]]''' is an obvious and strong choice.

Defending the pawn with '''[[Chess Opening Theory/1. e4/1...e5/2. d4/2...exd4/3. c3/3...dxc3/4. Bc4/4...cxb2/5. Bxb2/5...Nf6/6. Nc3|6. Nc3]]''' or, if you don't want to block the bishop's diagonal, with '''[[Chess Opening Theory/1. e4/1...e5/2. d4/2...exd4/3. c3/3...dxc3/4. Bc4/4...cxb2/5. Bxb2/5...Nf6/6. Nd2|6. Nd2]]''' are less popular.  Although developing pieces can't be bad, if Black finds the accurate ...d5 in response then the pawn is getting blown up anyway.

'''[[Chess Opening Theory/1. e4/1...e5/2. d4/2...exd4/3. c3/3...dxc3/4. Bc4/4...cxb2/5. Bxb2/5...Nf6/6. Nf3|6. Nf3]]''' is just a bit too normal to be an optimal move in this crazy line. The moves Black needs to find in response are correspondingly quite normal ones, like castling kingside.

'''[[Chess Opening Theory/1. e4/1...e5/2. d4/2...exd4/3. c3/3...dxc3/4. Bc4/4...cxb2/5. Bxb2/5...Nf6/6. Qb3|6. Qb3]]''' is obviously devastating if Black were forced to capture on e4, which she isn't. The immediate tactical problem for White is that 6...d5! now can't be met with 7.e5, because the White queen would find herself badly placed after 7...dxc4 8.exf6. 

The "forcing" 6. Bxf6 is a terrible, horrible, no good, very bad move. Apart from giving up the bishop pair, the Black queen that appears on f6 will target a1 (forcing the passive 7.Nd2) and also f2 in combination with a bishop on c5.

'''<big>6.e5</big>''' is the most popular and strongest move for White.{{ChessMid}}{{Chess Opening  Theory/Footer}}
title: Chess Opening Theory/1. e4/1...e5/2. d4/2...exd4/3. c3/3...dxc3/4. Bc4/4...cxb2/5. Bxb2/5...Nf6
